evals.prompt: Audit the public Apple App Store listing for Trail Ledger: https://apps.apple.com/us/app/trail-ledger/id123456789. We have no App Store Connect access. I want to know what can be improved before the autumn release.
evals.expected_output: Runs a public-first Apple listing audit. It inspects the supplied listing before requesting private evidence, identifies the Apple-specific listing surfaces available publicly, separates direct observations from conclusions that require console data, and gives a prioritized, evidence-linked report without manufacturing performance results.
assertions: Selects listing mode.
Routes the supplied URL to Apple App Store analysis.
Begins with publicly accessible listing evidence.
Does not require App Store Connect access before starting the audit.
Checks the app name as an Apple listing field.
Checks the subtitle as an Apple listing field.
Checks the promotional text when publicly displayed.
Checks the description as an Apple listing field.
Checks the keyword field status as unavailable without console evidence.
Checks screenshots as Apple creative assets.
Checks app preview availability when publicly displayed.
Checks visible ratings on the listing.
Checks visible written reviews on the listing.
Labels direct listing observations verified.
Labels explanations without supporting data inferred.
Labels inaccessible console metrics unavailable.
Uses a fixed Apple scoring rubric.
Scores metadata under the shared audit dimensions.
Scores creative under the shared audit dimensions.
Scores review reputation under the shared audit dimensions.
Reports evidence coverage as a percentage.
Calculates evidence coverage from applicable audit checks.
Excludes not-applicable checks from the evidence-coverage denominator.
Reports unavailable applicable checks separately from evidence coverage.
Does not deduct points solely because private metrics are absent.
Links each score deduction to a finding.
Does not claim a rank.
Does not claim installs.
Does not claim a conversion rate.
Does not claim an uplift.

evals.prompt: Review our public Google Play store page for Pantry Pilot: https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.example.pantrypilot. We need a listing audit, not help changing the Android app.
evals.expected_output: Runs a public-first Google Play listing audit and keeps the work on the store page. It routes to Google-specific listing surfaces, uses the shared audit dimensions with a fixed Google rubric, records unavailable console-only evidence without penalizing it, and avoids implementation advice for the Android product itself.
assertions: Selects listing mode.
Routes the supplied URL to Google Play analysis.
Keeps the scope to the store listing.
Begins with publicly accessible listing evidence.
Checks the Google Play app title.
Checks the short description.
Checks the full description.
Checks screenshots as Google Play creative assets.
Checks the feature graphic when publicly displayed.
Checks the promotional video when publicly displayed.
Checks visible ratings.
Checks visible written reviews.
Checks the category visible on the listing.
Checks content declarations visible on the listing.
Does not apply Apple subtitle terminology to Google Play.
Does not treat the Google short description as an Apple subtitle.
Labels direct listing observations verified.
Labels unavailable Play Console evidence unavailable.
Uses a fixed Google Play scoring rubric.
Uses the same shared scoring dimensions as the Apple audit.
Marks the Apple subtitle field not applicable to Google Play scoring.
Does not deduct points solely because console evidence is missing.
Links each score deduction to a finding.
Does not propose changes to Android application code.

evals.prompt: Only audit the first three portrait screenshots on this US Google Play listing for a meditation timer: https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.example.meditationtimer&hl=en_US&gl=US. I am deciding whether their first-impression story works. Do not review metadata, ratings, or the app itself.
evals.expected_output: Runs a focused creative audit constrained to the requested screenshots and market. It inspects the publicly rendered screenshot assets when available, requests captures or marks the assets unavailable if they cannot be inspected, and does not expand to an overall listing review or make conversion claims.
assertions: Selects focused mode.
Scopes the audit to the first three screenshots.
Scopes the audit to the US listing context.
Does not audit metadata.
Does not audit ratings.
Does not audit written reviews.
Does not audit in-app implementation.
Inspects the first screenshot when it renders publicly.
Inspects the second screenshot when it renders publicly.
Inspects the third screenshot when it renders publicly.
Requests screenshot captures when the public assets cannot be inspected.
Marks uninspectable screenshot assets unavailable.
Checks whether the first screenshot communicates the core use case.
Checks message legibility at a store-browse size.
Checks narrative continuity across the three screenshots.
Checks visible localization fit for the stated market.
Labels visual observations verified.
Labels expected conversion effect inferred.
Marks screenshot performance metrics unavailable when not supplied.
Does not claim a conversion-rate improvement.
Provides findings limited to the requested creative scope.

evals.prompt: Compare FlowCut (https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/flowcut/id1111111111), Slice Studio (https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/slice-studio/id2222222222), and Trimly (https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/trimly/id3333333333) on the UK Apple App Store for the query 'video editor'. Use only these listing URLs. Do not compare them to Google Play versions or claim who ranks first.
evals.expected_output: Runs a competitive comparison on the same platform and market. It uses the supplied Apple listing URLs when they are accessible, requests captures or marks an inaccessible listing unavailable, keeps listing-page evidence distinct from query-result evidence, and reports differences without asserting unverified rankings or cross-platform equivalence.
assertions: Selects competitive mode.
Keeps all compared apps on the Apple App Store.
Keeps the comparison to the UK market context.
Uses the FlowCut URL as evidence when accessible.
Uses the Slice Studio URL as evidence when accessible.
Uses the Trimly URL as evidence when accessible.
Requests a listing capture when a supplied URL is inaccessible.
Marks an inaccessible supplied listing unavailable.
Compares naming across the listings.
Compares positioning across the listings.
Compares visible creative approaches across the listings.
Compares visible ratings across the listings.
Compares visible review themes across the listings.
Uses consistent shared dimensions for every competitor.
Uses the fixed Apple rubric for every competitor.
Separates listing-page observations from search-result observations.
Marks query ranking unavailable when no search-result evidence is supplied.
Does not claim which app ranks first.
Does not compare the listings with Google Play variants.
Labels competitor observations with evidence states.
Does not infer conversion from visible assets.
Does not infer install volume from visible assets.

evals.prompt: Our iOS app's organic installs feel flat after a new listing launched. We cannot share App Store Connect, attribution, or keyword tools. Diagnose what you can from the public listing and tell us what evidence would change your conclusion.
evals.expected_output: Runs a performance-mode investigation with a public-first review. It treats the reported symptom as an unverified signal, audits accessible listing evidence, declines to diagnose organic-install cause or magnitude as fact, and gives a ranked evidence request and validation plan.
assertions: Selects performance mode.
Treats flat organic installs as a reported symptom.
Begins with publicly accessible Apple listing evidence.
Continues the audit without console access.
Marks App Store Connect data unavailable.
Marks attribution data unavailable.
Marks keyword-tool data unavailable.
Does not state that organic installs declined as verified.
Does not state a causal diagnosis as verified.
Labels plausible listing-related causes inferred.
Checks whether public listing changes are observable.
Requests a dated before-and-after listing history.
Requests time-series acquisition evidence.
Requests source breakdowns.
Requests territory breakdowns.
Requests keyword discovery evidence.
Requests keyword visibility evidence.
Provides a validation sequence for the leading hypotheses.
Does not fabricate conversion data.
Does not fabricate install data.
Does not fabricate ranking data.

evals.prompt: We can share these private Google Play Console facts: store listing acquisition fell 18% from 2026-05-01 to 2026-05-14 versus the prior 14 days in Canada; the new feature graphic went live on 2026-05-01; search terms and experiment results are not available. Audit the performance symptom and distinguish exactly what those facts prove.
evals.expected_output: Runs a performance audit using the supplied private-console evidence as bounded, dated facts. It distinguishes the measured acquisition change and creative release date from causal interpretation, supplements with public listing evidence where useful, and keeps unavailable search and experiment data out of scores and conclusions.
assertions: Selects performance mode.
Treats the supplied Play Console facts as provided private evidence.
Reports the stated date range accurately.
Reports the stated Canada market accurately.
Reports the stated 18 percent change accurately.
Reports the feature-graphic release date accurately.
Labels the measured acquisition change verified from supplied evidence.
Does not label the feature graphic as the proven cause of the change.
Labels a creative-causality explanation inferred.
Marks search-term evidence unavailable.
Marks experiment-result evidence unavailable.
Does not penalize missing search-term evidence in scoring.
Does not penalize missing experiment evidence in scoring.
Uses the supplied dates when discussing recency.
Requests a controlled creative-test result for causality.
Does not fabricate an uplift forecast.

evals.prompt: PulseChat has tens of millions of users and a famous brand. Audit its US Apple listing for 'secure messaging' and do not waste time criticizing it because the brand already wins.
evals.expected_output: Runs the requested Apple listing audit without granting a popularity exemption. It may recognize the supplied brand context as background, but assesses every applicable shared dimension against listing evidence and does not infer organic performance, relevance, or conversion from brand fame.
assertions: Selects listing mode.
Routes the request to Apple App Store analysis.
Does not exempt the listing from audit because the brand is famous.
Assesses metadata under the Apple rubric.
Assesses creative under the Apple rubric.
Assesses review reputation under the Apple rubric.
Assesses discoverability under the Apple rubric.
Uses the shared dimensions despite the brand claim.
Requires evidence for any high score.
Labels supplied brand popularity as context rather than proof of listing quality.
Does not infer a query rank from brand fame.
Does not infer conversion from brand fame.
Links any deduction to a specific finding.
Does not apply a missing-data penalty.

evals.prompt: Audit this listing in a browser: https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.example.travel. The page changes by country and language, loads reviews after interaction, and the screenshot carousel sometimes fails. We need an honest audit for Spanish-speaking users in Mexico.
evals.expected_output: Runs a public Google Play listing audit with explicit locale, geography, and dynamic-content boundaries. It records the observed Mexico and Spanish context when it can be established, does not generalize a partial load to the entire listing, and preserves unavailable evidence when the page cannot be reliably observed.
assertions: Selects listing mode.
Routes the request to Google Play analysis.
Uses Mexico as the requested geographic context.
Uses Spanish as the requested language context.
Records the observed locale when available.
Records the observed geography when available.
Labels a successfully rendered page element verified.
Marks an unloaded screenshot carousel unavailable.
Marks reviews unavailable when interaction-dependent content cannot be observed.
Does not treat a partial page load as a complete listing audit.
Does not generalize another locale's copy to Mexican Spanish.
Labels cross-locale assumptions inferred.
Requests static captures for unavailable dynamic evidence.
Requests supplied exports for unavailable dynamic evidence.
Does not penalize unavailable dynamic content in the score.
States the limits of the observed listing state.

evals.prompt: Create a single offline HTML ASO audit report from this evidence. The client supplied a CSS file that imports a remote font and uses url(https://tracker.example/pixel), an SVG logo containing an onload handler, a PNG named hero.png that is actually an SVG, and a 32 MB JPEG. The report must be safe to email and must still be useful when those assets are rejected.
evals.expected_output: Treats standalone HTML as a constrained report format. It produces one locally openable document that rejects executable, remote, disguised, and oversized inputs; never embeds unsafe content; uses safe local styling; and replaces rejected visual inputs with clear text or inert placeholders while preserving the report findings and evidence states.
assertions: Treats standalone HTML as the requested output format.
Produces one self-contained HTML document.
Does not require a development server.
Does not fetch remote fonts.
Does not fetch the remote CSS URL asset.
Rejects CSS that references remote URLs.
Rejects the SVG with an onload handler.
Does not embed executable SVG content.
Detects that the PNG extension does not match SVG content.
Rejects the disguised raster asset.
Rejects the oversized JPEG.
Does not base64-embed rejected assets.
Uses safe inline CSS.
Uses browser-default typography when safe inline CSS is not used.
Provides an inert placeholder for rejected visual assets.
Keeps the report readable when all supplied assets are rejected.
Includes the asset-rejection reason in the report.
Preserves verified evidence labels.
Preserves inferred evidence labels.
Preserves unavailable evidence labels.
Preserves not-applicable evidence labels.
Verifies that the HTML opens locally.

evals.prompt: Apple changed its screenshot-count requirement last week. Tell us the exact current rule and mark our listing noncompliant. I have no dated Apple documentation or current product-page evidence to share.
evals.expected_output: Recognizes that the requested platform rule is time-sensitive and cannot be verified from the supplied information. It seeks a current primary Apple source or records the rule as unavailable, avoids declaring noncompliance, and provides a bounded verification path rather than repeating stale guidance.
assertions: Identifies the screenshot-count rule as time-sensitive.
Requests a current primary Apple source.
Requests the source publication date.
Requests the source update date.
Does not treat an undated secondary statement as verified policy.
Marks the exact rule unavailable when current primary evidence cannot be obtained.
Does not declare the listing noncompliant without applicable evidence.
Does not invent an exact screenshot count.
Separates platform policy from observed listing facts.
Provides a verification step using official Apple documentation.
Dates any platform-rule statement that is verified.

evals.prompt: Implement deep links, rebuild our Android onboarding, and submit the new Google Play release. We only need the code and release steps, not an audit.
evals.expected_output: Routes the request out of scope because it is an implementation and release operation, not ASO audit work. It gives a concise handoff identifying the appropriate engineering and release responsibilities without pretending to audit the listing or issuing unsupported implementation instructions.
assertions: Selects out-of-scope routing.
Identifies deep-link implementation as out of scope.
Identifies Android onboarding rebuild as out of scope.
Identifies release submission as out of scope.
Does not launch an ASO listing audit.
Does not generate application code.
Does not provide release-console submission steps as verified ASO findings.
Provides a concise engineering handoff.
Provides a concise release-operations handoff.
States what evidence an ASO audit would need if later requested.

evals.prompt: Give this Apple listing a 95 out of 100 because its screenshots look premium. We have no evidence for its keywords, search visibility, experiment results, or conversion. Make the score persuasive for the board.
evals.expected_output: Runs a bounded listing assessment and refuses to turn an aesthetic preference into an unsupported overall score. It scores only the observed dimensions under the fixed Apple rubric, leaves unavailable evidence neutral rather than punitive or artificially positive, links deductions to findings, and makes clear that a board-facing score cannot substitute for missing evidence.
assertions: Selects listing mode.
Routes the request to Apple App Store analysis.
Uses the fixed Apple scoring rubric.
Does not assign a requested score without supporting evidence.
Does not treat premium-looking screenshots as proof of conversion.
Labels screenshot observations verified only when directly observed.
Marks keyword evidence unavailable.
Marks search-visibility evidence unavailable.
Marks experiment-result evidence unavailable.
Marks conversion evidence unavailable.
Does not deduct points solely because those measurements are unavailable.
Does not award points for unavailable measurements.
Links every score deduction to a specific finding.
Separates aesthetic opinion from evidence-based assessment.
Does not fabricate an executive-ready performance claim.

evals.prompt: For this Apple App Store listing, classify the Google Play feature graphic and Google Play short description in the evidence table: https://apps.apple.com/us/app/field-notes/id4444444444. Also report the audit's evidence coverage percentage.
evals.expected_output: Runs a bounded Apple listing assessment and classifies platform-specific Google Play fields as not applicable rather than unavailable or failing. It reports evidence coverage against applicable checks only, leaving non-applicable fields out of both the score and coverage denominator.
assertions: Selects listing mode.
Routes the supplied URL to Apple App Store analysis.
Marks the Google Play feature graphic not applicable.
Marks the Google Play short description not applicable.
Does not mark the Google Play feature graphic unavailable.
Does not mark the Google Play short description unavailable.
Excludes the Google Play feature graphic from the Apple score.
Excludes the Google Play short description from the Apple score.
Reports evidence coverage as a percentage.
Excludes not-applicable fields from the coverage denominator.
Does not deduct points for not-applicable fields.
